legacyFallback.description: Add either query parameter to the app URL to restore the previous single-question checkpoints for development testing.

heldOutHandling: Runtime learner-facing copy replaces unexplained held-out with set-aside validation examples, new validation examples, or validation examples the model did not train on.
Identifier-level strings may preserve heldout/held-out only for CSS/storage compatibility and are not learner-facing.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Reason that an LLM generates response tokens by using learned weights and current context, without implying awareness or perfect search.
secondaryObjectives: Distinguish fluent generated behavior from conscious understanding.
Separate inference from training and search/database retrieval.
Connect prompt, response, token, weight, and inference in one model-centered trace.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Inference uses fixed learned weights and temporary context to score, sample, append, and repeat next tokens.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: A fluent LLM must be conscious.
An LLM is a database or search engine that knows every file.
A normal prompt durably teaches the model new weights.

draft-what-is-llm-q1.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem is too curriculum-centered or definition-like; it should make the learner reason through model behavior.

draft-what-is-llm-q2.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

learningObjectiveReview.questionCountRationale: What Is an LLM? keeps the reviewed v0.27.9 model-thinking question count for this first-twelve activation pass.

kept: Q1 mechanism stem and core distractor set.
revised: Q1 correct answer shortened.
Q2 stem and correct answer revised for model-literate judgment.

questions.question: When an LLM answers a prompt by generating one token at a time, what is the model doing at each step?

choices.text: It scores possible next tokens from the current context, selects one, appends it, and repeats.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. The model is using fixed learned weights and temporary context to make the next-token step.

choices.text: It reads with conscious understanding, then writes the whole answer at once.
choices.feedback: Not quite. That gives the model too much mind. Fluent behavior can happen without awareness.
choices.representedMisconception: conscious mind vs fluent model behavior
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because fluent text can sound like a human explanation.

choices.text: It searches a hidden database and copies the best matching passage.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Search can be added around a model, but a plain LLM generates from context and learned weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because broad model knowledge can feel like search.

choices.text: It changes its weights each time the user sends a new prompt.
choices.feedback: That would be true only if a separate training process updated weights. Ordinary inference usually leaves weights fixed.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the model may seem to adapt within a conversation.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner traces the next-token loop rather than recalling the card definition.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports the core model-literacy distinction among context, response tokens, and fixed weights.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Kept the v0.27.8 stem.
Shortened the correct answer and used selects one because sampling has not yet been introduced.
Kept conscious-reader, search/database, and changing-weights distractors.

questions.question: An LLM gives a fluent explanation of a poem. What should a model-literate learner conclude?

choices.text: It generated response tokens from context and learned patterns; fluency does not prove awareness.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. This is the fluent-behavior-without-awareness boundary Prompt Life wants learners to hold.

choices.text: It must have inner awareness because the explanation sounds human.
choices.feedback: Not quite. That gives the model too much mind. Fluent behavior can happen without awareness.
choices.representedMisconception: conscious mind vs fluent model behavior
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because human-like language invites human-like interpretation.

choices.text: It must have found a hidden perfect source and copied the interpretation.
choices.feedback: Close, but this describes search or retrieval better than a plain LLM response. A fluent answer is not the same as cited evidence.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because polished answers can feel copied from a source.

choices.text: The poem prompt permanently taught the base model a new skill.
choices.feedback: That would require training. A normal prompt can shape temporary context without durably updating weights.
choices.representedMisconception: prompt/context change vs durable weight update
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because earlier context can shape later replies in a chat.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner diagnoses a real interpretive trap: fluent explanation without inner awareness.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Demystifies model fluency without implying consciousness.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Revised the stem exactly toward model-literate learner judgment.
Kept fluent-without-awareness framing in plain language.

cleanupFromV0279: No learner-facing checkpoint logic changes from v0.27.9.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Use the AI family map to reason about what kind of generative system an LLM is and what it is not.
secondaryObjectives: Distinguish AI, machine learning, deep learning, generative AI, LLMs, diffusion models, and symbolic AI.
Avoid treating all AI or all generative AI as ChatGPT-like LLMs.
Recognize that different generative systems can produce different media through different mechanisms.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: LLMs are deep-learning generative AI systems focused on language/code; diffusion is a different generative family.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: All AI systems are LLMs.
All generative AI works like ChatGPT.
AI and machine learning are interchangeable labels.

draft-where-llms-fit-q1.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem is too curriculum-centered or definition-like; it should make the learner reason through model behavior.

draft-where-llms-fit-q2.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

learningObjectiveReview.questionCountRationale: Where LLMs Fit keeps the reviewed v0.27.9 model-thinking question count for this first-twelve activation pass.

revised: Q1 denoising wording.
Q2 shorter correct answer and length-balanced distractors.

questions.question: A campus tool summarizes text with an LLM, while another creates images by denoising a noisy pattern. What does that show?

choices.text: Generative AI is a family; LLMs and diffusion models are different branches.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Generative AI is a family, and different branches can use different mechanisms.

choices.text: All generative AI tools are LLMs because ChatGPT is generative AI.
choices.feedback: Not quite. An LLM is one kind of generative AI, not the whole category.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because ChatGPT is many learners’ first generative AI example.

choices.text: The image tool is not AI unless it generates language tokens.
choices.feedback: Close, but image models can be AI without using the LLM text-token loop.
choices.representedMisconception: LLM output vs broader generative outputs
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because this app starts with language models.

choices.text: AI and machine learning are the same label, so the branches do not matter.
choices.feedback: This choice collapses useful categories. Machine learning is a major branch inside the broader AI field.
choices.representedMisconception: AI vs machine learning category boundary
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because public language often blurs these words.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner compares two generative model families by mechanism and output type.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Prevents overgeneralizing LLM lessons to every AI system.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Changed denoising noise to denoising a noisy pattern.
Kept the generative-family versus LLM/diffusion branch distinction.

questions.question: When people say an LLM sits inside the broader AI family, which category map is most useful?

choices.text: AI is broad; machine learning learns from data; deep learning uses layers; generative AI creates outputs; LLMs focus on language/code.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. The map keeps overlapping terms useful without making them interchangeable.

choices.text: AI means LLM; machine learning, deep learning, and generative AI are just extra names.
choices.feedback: Not quite. LLMs are an important branch, not the root of the AI tree.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because LLMs currently dominate public attention.

choices.text: Rule-based AI, machine learning, and LLMs all work by following the same written rules.
choices.feedback: Close, but rule-based systems rely on explicit rules while learned models use patterns shaped from data.
choices.representedMisconception: symbolic AI vs learned model boundary
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because all software runs instructions somewhere.

choices.text: Diffusion models, LLMs, and search engines all use the same language-token loop.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Diffusion often denoises patterns; LLM text generation usually appends response tokens.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because all can appear inside AI products.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner uses a category map to avoid treating AI, app, and model mechanisms as interchangeable.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports precise institutional conversations about AI systems.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Shortened the correct answer while preserving the category map.
Made distractors closer in length and structure.

questions.question: A chatbot product includes an LLM, a search tool, safety filters, and a user interface. What is the LLM?

choices.text: The language-generating model inside the larger product.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. A product may wrap an LLM with tools, policies, and interface pieces.

choices.text: The whole app, including every tool and button.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The product can include an LLM, but the LLM is not every surrounding tool and screen.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because users experience the whole product as one AI thing.

choices.text: The safety policy that blocks some outputs.
choices.feedback: Close, but a safety policy shapes behavior around the model; it is not the language model itself.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because safety behavior is visible in the product.

choices.text: The database or search index the app may call.
choices.feedback: This describes a retrieval or search component. The LLM generates language from context and learned weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because some chatbot products include search.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner separates the model from the product stack around it.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Prevents treating ChatGPT, LLM, app, search, guardrail, and interface as the same thing.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Added the requested model-vs-product checkpoint question.
Used the requested correct answer and wrong-answer feedback boundaries.

cleanupFromV0279: No learner-facing checkpoint logic changes from v0.27.9.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Reason through the difference between explicit rules and learned weights, while recognizing that real AI products may combine both.
secondaryObjectives: Distinguish symbolic/rule-based systems from deep-learning systems.
Explain loss as the training signal that guides weight adjustment.
Avoid treating LLM fluency as consciousness or treating hybrid apps as pure learned models.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Deep learning learns from examples by predicting, measuring loss, and adjusting weights; symbolic AI relies on explicit rules.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: LLMs are only hand-coded rulebooks.
Fluent model output proves conscious reasoning.
Any rule, filter, or retrieval step means there is no learned model.

draft-history-q1.rationale: The boundary is right, but the stem should ask the learner to compare actual system behavior.

draft-history-q2.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

draft-history-q3.rationale: The misconception target is useful, but the stem should become an applied model-thinking scenario.

learningObjectiveReview.questionCountRationale: Rationalists vs Empiricists keeps the reviewed v0.27.9 model-thinking question count for this first-twelve activation pass.

kept: Q1 rule-based versus learned weights scenario.
revised: Q2 replaced weak retrieval distractor with confidence/probability distractor.
Q3 correct answer and feedback tightened.

questions.question: If a system solves a task only by following hand-written if-then rules, how is it different from an LLM?

choices.text: The rule-based system applies explicit rules; an LLM’s fluency mostly comes from learned weights shaped by examples.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. LLMs mostly belong to the learned-patterns tradition, even when products around them use rules.

choices.text: The rule-based system measures loss and updates weights after every task.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Loss and weight updates belong to training learned models, not to a pure if-then rule system.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because loss is nearby in the deep-learning explanation.

choices.text: The LLM has conscious reasoning, while the rule-based system has none.
choices.feedback: That gives the LLM too much mind. The key boundary here is learned weights versus explicit rules.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because LLM language can sound like reasoning from the inside.

choices.text: There is no difference; both systems are just written rulebooks.
choices.feedback: Close, but LLM behavior is not mainly written as explicit if-then rules; it is shaped by training.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both systems can produce predictable outputs.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner compares two system designs rather than reciting intellectual history.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Builds the architecture boundary needed for training/inference distinctions.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Kept the question mostly as-is.
Tightened the correct answer to emphasize explicit rules versus learned weights.

questions.question: During deep-learning training, why does the system measure loss?

choices.text: Loss measures prediction error so an optimizer can adjust weights.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Loss is the numerical training signal, not a feeling or truth meter.

choices.text: Loss tells the model whether a generated answer is morally good.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Loss is a numerical error signal; alignment and policy questions need additional methods.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because loss sounds like a judgment word.

choices.text: Loss is the model’s confidence score for the next token during generation.
choices.feedback: Close, but confidence or probability shapes generation. Loss measures training error so weights can be adjusted.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both are numerical signals related to prediction.

choices.text: Loss writes the final answer after the model has finished thinking.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Loss is used during training; it is not the model writing a response.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because learners may treat every internal step as answer writing.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner identifies the causal role of loss inside training.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Loss is needed for badge-worthy understanding of how weights change.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Kept the stem.
Replaced the weak retrieval distractor with the requested confidence/probability distractor.

questions.question: A university AI app uses an LLM plus policy filters, retrieval, and hand-written rules. What should a model-literate user conclude?

choices.text: Modern AI products can combine learned models, rules, tools, and policies; the LLM inside is still a learned-pattern model.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. The product can be hybrid even when the LLM itself is learned from data.

choices.text: Any rule in the app means the LLM is no longer trained from data.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Rules can wrap or guide a learned model without erasing the learned weights inside it.
choices.representedMisconception: hybrid system vs pure symbolic replacement
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because rule-based pieces can be visible in product behavior.

choices.text: Any fluent answer means the app is consciously reasoning.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. The app can combine tools and still lack conscious understanding.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the whole app may feel conversational.

choices.text: Retrieval or filters permanently fine-tune the model on every use.
choices.feedback: Close, but retrieval and filters can shape the current response without durably changing model weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the app appears to adapt to local material.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner reasons about real product stacks, not just model families.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports institutional AI literacy: model, wrapper, tool, and policy are not the same thing.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Kept the university app scenario.
Revised the correct answer and feedback to emphasize hybrid products and learned-pattern LLMs.

cleanupFromV0279: No learner-facing checkpoint logic changes from v0.27.9.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Trace training as the durable weight-changing loop and separate it from ordinary inference, sampling, retrieval, and context changes.
secondaryObjectives: Identify loss and weight update as the learning step.
Explain why a normal chat does not usually update model weights.
Trace example, prediction, loss, and update as the durable training path.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Training uses examples and loss to update parameters; inference uses fixed weights and temporary activations.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: Every prompt trains the model.
Training is the same as sampling or generation.
Weight updates, context changes, and retrieved text are interchangeable.

draft-training-q1.rationale: The training/inference boundary is essential, but the pilot should ask about a concrete training moment.

draft-training-q2.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

draft-training-q3.rationale: The misconception target is useful, but the stem should become an applied model-thinking scenario.

draft-training-q4.rationale: The relationship target is useful, but the stem should ask the learner to trace a concrete process.

learningObjectiveReview.questionCountRationale: Training keeps the reviewed v0.27.9 model-thinking question count for this first-twelve activation pass.

revised: Q1 replaced softmax-truth distractor.
Q2 rewritten as a positive stem.
Q4 replaced diffusion distractor with user-correction misconception.

questions.question: During training, the model predicts a target token and gets it wrong. What makes that moment learning rather than ordinary inference?

choices.text: The training process uses the loss signal to update weights.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. The durable weight update is what makes this training.

choices.text: The model appends the mistaken token to a normal live response.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Appending a response token is inference behavior; training uses loss to change weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because generation also chooses and appends tokens.

choices.text: The prompt becomes durable memory stored inside the model’s weights.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Context can shape the current chat without becoming a weight update.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because chat context can feel remembered.

choices.text: The model treats the most likely answer as automatically true.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Likelihood is not truth, and this training moment matters because loss can drive a weight update.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because likely answers can sound authoritative.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner locates the precise causal step that changes the model.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Core badge distinction: training changes weights; inference uses them.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Kept the stem and correct answer.
Replaced the premature softmax-truth distractor with the requested likelihood-vs-truth distractor.

questions.question: A student asks a chatbot one question during ordinary use. Which statement is usually true?

choices.text: The model uses fixed weights and temporary context; the chat usually does not rewrite the weights.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Normal chat inference can use context, but it usually does not rewrite weights.

choices.text: The chat permanently updates the base model’s weights.
choices.feedback: Not quite. That would require a training process, not ordinary inference.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the model appears to adapt to the user.

choices.text: The model stores the prompt as durable memory inside its weights.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Context can shape the current chat without becoming a weight update.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the model can refer to earlier chat context.

choices.text: The optimizer trains the model after every response.
choices.feedback: Close, but optimizers update weights during training, not after every normal response.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because training and inference both involve model computation.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner reasons from a familiar chat scenario to the durable/temporary boundary.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Reduces the myth that every user chat trains the base model.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Rewrote the negative stem as the requested positive stem.
Used the requested correct answer and distractor set.

questions.question: Why can training change how the model behaves tomorrow, while inference usually cannot?

choices.text: Training changes parameters that future runs reuse; inference uses temporary computations for the current run.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Durable parameters carry forward; inference-time states usually do not.

choices.text: Inference is where the optimizer changes weights for future runs.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Optimizer-driven weight updates belong to training, not ordinary inference.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both training and inference run model computations.

choices.text: Training only changes the visible prompt text for one response.
choices.feedback: Close, but prompt changes are temporary input changes; training changes learned numbers.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because prompt text clearly changes a response.

choices.text: Training guarantees that every future answer will be true.
choices.feedback: This choice overclaims. Training can improve behavior, but it does not guarantee truth.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because training sounds like making the model better.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner connects durability to parameters reused by future inference.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Strengthens durable-versus-temporary literacy.
authoringNotes.reviewStatus: kept from v0.27.8 with light wording cleanup

humanReviewNotesApplied: Kept the v0.27.8 stem and correct answer.

questions.question: In a training loop, which sequence is the durable learning path?

choices.text: Example -> prediction -> loss -> weight update.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. That is the weight-changing path.

choices.text: Prompt -> response token -> append -> next response token.
choices.feedback: Close, but that sequence describes inference/generation, not training.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because this is the model’s generation loop.

choices.text: Search -> outside passage -> current context -> answer.
choices.feedback: That describes a search or retrieval step. It can add temporary context; it does not by itself update weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because outside text can improve an answer.

choices.text: User correction -> better answer -> permanent learning inside the same chat.
choices.feedback: Not quite. A correction can steer the current conversation, but durable learning requires a training process.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because a correction can improve the current conversation.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner chooses the path with the durable update, not another plausible AI sequence.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Prepares learners to distinguish training, context, and generation later.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Kept the sequence idea and correct answer.
Removed the premature diffusion distractor.
Added the requested user-correction distractor.

cleanupFromV0279: No learner-facing checkpoint logic changes from v0.27.9.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Reason that pretraining is broad durable pattern learning from next-token prediction over large datasets, not perfect recall or live browsing.
secondaryObjectives: Connect next-token targets, loss, weight updates, and broad model capability.
Separate pretraining from RAG, fine-tuning, inference, and database lookup.
Explain how broad data can shape weights without installing conscious understanding.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Pretraining repeats the training loop at large scale, changing weights before normal use.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: Pretraining gives perfect recall of every source.
Pretraining happens during each user chat.
Fine-tuning or RAG explains all broad model knowledge.

draft-pretraining-q1.rationale: The durable weight-change idea is correct, but the pilot should name the pretraining signal and model mechanism.

draft-pretraining-q2.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

draft-pretraining-q3.rationale: The misconception target is useful, but the stem should become an applied model-thinking scenario.

draft-pretraining-q4.rationale: The relationship target is useful, but the stem should ask the learner to trace a concrete process.

learningObjectiveReview.questionCountRationale: Pretraining keeps the reviewed v0.27.9 model-thinking question count for this first-twelve activation pass.

revised: Q2 avoids conscious-recall wording.
Q3 replaced citation-list distractor.
Q4 avoids seems-to-know stem and clarifies source-like output is not evidence.

questions.question: During pretraining, what signal teaches the model broad language patterns across many examples?

choices.text: Repeated prediction error/loss from next-token targets changes weights over many training steps.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining scales the training loop across many examples.

choices.text: A user conversation at deployment permanently rewrites the model.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Pretraining happens before normal use; an ordinary chat usually does not rewrite weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the model can seem to adapt inside a chat.

choices.text: A human writes every rule the model will follow.
choices.feedback: Close, but pretraining is learned from examples, not a complete rulebook written by hand.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because software often has hand-written rules.

choices.text: The model stores a perfect copy of every source document.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Pretraining shapes weights; it is not perfect document storage.
choices.representedMisconception: pattern learning vs perfect source recall
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because source data influences the model.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner identifies the training signal that builds broad capability.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Connects next-token prediction to durable weight learning.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Kept the v0.27.8 stem and correct answer mostly as-is.

questions.question: Why can pretraining create broad capability without making the model a perfect library of its sources?

choices.text: It shapes statistical patterns in weights; it does not create a searchable copy of every source.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining can shape broad ability without turning the model into a perfect source library.

choices.text: It gives the model direct access to every private file on demand.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Training data exposure is not the same as access to every private file.
choices.representedMisconception: training data vs universal file access
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because broad training data can sound like broad access.

choices.text: It inserts the original source into every future prompt context.
choices.feedback: Close, but adding source text to context is different from pretraining. Pretraining changes weights before use.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because source material can sometimes be added to a prompt.

choices.text: It happens only after a user asks a specific question.
choices.feedback: Pretraining happens before deployment. The user prompt later uses the pretrained weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because learners meet the model only during use.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner reasons about what weights can and cannot contain.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports nuanced memory, privacy, and retrieval literacy.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Revised the correct answer to avoid conscious-recall framing.
Used searchable copy of every source wording.

questions.question: If pretraining examples include many styles and task shapes, what can change inside the model?

choices.text: Weights can shift so future prompts activate more useful language/code patterns.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining changes reusable model structure before later prompts arrive.

choices.text: The model’s current chat context becomes permanently larger.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Context is temporary input; pretraining changes reusable weights before later prompts arrive.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because context also affects output.

choices.text: Only the app’s buttons, labels, or interface change.
choices.feedback: This describes product UI changes, not the model’s learned parameters.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because users meet models through apps.

choices.text: Only a safety policy is added around the model.
choices.feedback: Close, but a policy can shape a product around the model; pretraining changes the model’s reusable weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because products often include safety behavior.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner connects broad examples to future behavior through weights.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Builds a model-centered explanation for broad capability.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Replaced the citation-list distractor with current chat context becomes permanently larger.

questions.question: A base model produces fact-like text about public topics. What is the model-literate explanation?

choices.text: Pretraining shaped weights with patterns from data, so the model may generate fact-like text without retrieving the original page.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Source-like output can come from pretrained patterns, but it is not the same as cited evidence.

choices.text: It must be browsing the web by itself for every answer.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Some systems browse or retrieve, but a base model can also generate fact-like text from pretrained weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because web search is a familiar way to find facts.

choices.text: It must consciously remember reading those pages.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. It has learned weights, not conscious recollection.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the output can sound like human memory.

choices.text: Fine-tuning alone explains all broad model knowledge.
choices.feedback: Close, but fine-tuning is targeted shaping after broad pretraining; it does not usually explain the whole foundation.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because fine-tuning is another training phase.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner diagnoses fact-like generation without treating it as live source lookup.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports source literacy without implying omniscience.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Changed the stem to avoid seems to know.
Kept web browsing, conscious memory, and fine-tuning distractors.
Clarified that source-like output is not cited evidence.

cleanupFromV0279: No learner-facing checkpoint logic changes from v0.27.9.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Diagnose when a model has fit training examples too narrowly and explain why set-aside validation checks generalization.
secondaryObjectives: Separate training performance from performance on new examples.
Explain why validation data matters.
Recognize when targeted training can become too narrow.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Overfitting installs brittle patterns into weights; generalization means learned patterns transfer to unseen examples.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: Perfect training performance proves a model learned well.
Validation data is just more context.
Lower loss or memorization automatically guarantees truthful future answers.

draft-overfitting-generalization-q1.rationale: The overfitting sign is correct, but the pilot should use an applied train/validation scenario.

draft-overfitting-generalization-q2.rationale: The v0.27.7 stem asks for the card mechanism generically; it should name a concrete model action or boundary.

draft-overfitting-generalization-q3.rationale: The misconception target is useful, but the stem should become an applied model-thinking scenario.

learningObjectiveReview.questionCountRationale: Overfitting and Generalization keeps the reviewed v0.27.9 model-thinking question count for this first-twelve activation pass.

kept: Q1 and Q2 core stems and correct answers.
revised: Q1 replaced RAG distractor.
Q2 replaced context-window distractor.
Q3 rewritten without fine-tuning as the main frame.

questions.question: A model gets every training example right but fails on new validation examples. What problem is showing up?

choices.text: Overfitting: the model fit training examples too narrowly instead of learning patterns that generalize.
choices.feedback: Good diagnosis. The set-aside validation examples reveal whether the pattern transfers.

choices.text: Generalization: the model is working well on unseen cases.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Generalization means good performance on new examples; this scenario shows the opposite.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both terms describe training performance.

choices.text: Better training: the model has clearly learned the general rule.
choices.feedback: Not quite. If it fails on new validation examples, high training performance is not enough evidence of generalization.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because perfect training performance feels reassuring.

choices.text: Inference: the model is simply generating one token at a time.
choices.feedback: Close, but inference is normal model use; overfitting is a training/evaluation failure mode.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because all later model use involves inference.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner diagnoses overfitting from train/validation behavior.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Builds evaluation literacy: success on seen examples is not enough.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Kept the stem and correct answer.
Replaced the RAG distractor with better training.

questions.question: Why do model builders test on set-aside validation examples instead of only training examples?

choices.text: Set-aside validation examples checks whether learned patterns work on examples the model did not train on.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Validation is about transfer, not memorized training performance.

choices.text: Validation data is just extra training data used to lower loss.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Validation data is saved for testing to check transfer; it should not simply be another set of examples used to fit weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because validation examples can look like more examples.

choices.text: Training examples alone prove the model will work everywhere.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals the exact overfitting risk. Seen examples are not enough evidence.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because high training accuracy feels reassuring.

choices.text: A lower loss always proves every future answer is true.
choices.feedback: Close, but lower loss is not a truth guarantee, especially outside the tested examples.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because lower loss often signals improvement.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner connects evaluation design to model behavior on new cases.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports responsible interpretation of AI benchmarks and claims.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Kept the stem and correct answer.
Replaced the context-window distractor with validation-as-extra-training.

questions.question: A model trained heavily on one narrow answer template works on that template but struggles with varied new prompts. What should a model-literate learner suspect?

choices.text: It may have fit the narrow examples too closely instead of learning a pattern that generalizes.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Targeted training can help, but narrow training can also make behavior brittle.

choices.text: The prompts permanently rewrote the model during inference.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The issue is training behavior, not prompts rewriting weights during inference.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because prompts can change output behavior.

choices.text: The model is definitely conscious but choosing not to answer.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. The issue is brittle transfer, not intention.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because poor behavior can seem intentional.

choices.text: Rule-based AI is always better than learned models.
choices.feedback: This overgeneralizes. The issue is narrow training behavior, not proof that rules always beat learned models.
choices.representedMisconception: overfitting diagnosis vs symbolic superiority claim
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because brittleness can make rules feel safer.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner applies overfitting/generalization without relying on fine-tuning as the main frame.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Connects training choices to trustworthy human use.

humanReviewNotesApplied: Rewrote the stem without fine-tuning as the main frame.
Used the requested distractors and feedback boundaries.

cleanupFromV0279: Replaced learner-facing held-out wording with set-aside validation examples / validation examples the model did not train on.
Preserved the approved question logic and distractor structure.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Distinguish durable fine-tuning from temporary prompt, RAG, and sampling-time steering.
secondaryObjectives: Identify fine-tuning as additional training after pretraining.
Separate model-weight or adapter changes from current-context changes.
Explain why one base model can become many specialized assistants.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Fine-tuning changes weights or adapter behavior for future runs; prompting and RAG shape the current context; sampling chooses a token during generation.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: A better prompt is fine-tuning.
RAG permanently teaches the model.
Sampling is a durable adaptation step.
learningObjectiveReview.questionCountRationale: Three questions cover the durable/temporary boundary, a product scenario, and the relationship to alignment without overloading a focused card.

kept: Fine-tuning remains a durable post-pretraining shaping concept.
revised: Expanded checkpoint coverage beyond one definition-like question.
added: Boundary, applied scenario, and alignment-relationship questions.

questions.question: A team wants a support assistant to answer in a durable house style across future conversations. Which move is closest to fine-tuning?

choices.text: Run additional training that changes weights or adapter behavior for future responses.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Fine-tuning is durable shaping after pretraining.

choices.text: Write one clearer prompt for the current chat.
choices.feedback: Not quite. A prompt can steer the current run, but fine-tuning changes future behavior through training.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because prompts can strongly steer one answer.

choices.text: Retrieve one policy PDF into the current context.
choices.feedback: Close, but RAG adds temporary context. Fine-tuning is a training process that durably shapes behavior.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because retrieved text can improve one answer.

choices.text: Sample the next token from the current probability cloud.
choices.feedback: That describes decoding during inference. It chooses a token; it does not adapt the model for future runs.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because sampling is part of response generation.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner chooses the durable weight/adaptation path instead of temporary steering.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports the durable-versus-temporary model literacy boundary.

questions.question: A base model is adapted on thousands of institution-specific examples, then later answers new users in that style. What changed most directly?

choices.text: The adapted model’s weights or adapter behavior can carry that pattern into later inference runs.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Fine-tuning makes a reusable model change, not just a one-chat hint.

choices.text: Only the current context window got larger.
choices.feedback: Not quite. A larger context window is temporary input space; fine-tuning changes reusable behavior.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because context can also carry institution-specific text.

choices.text: The model became conscious of the institution’s norms.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. The behavior can be shaped without conscious commitment to norms.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the assistant may sound norm-aware.

choices.text: The model must search the institution’s files every time.
choices.feedback: Close, but fine-tuning and retrieval are different. Retrieval may be added, but fine-tuned behavior can persist in weights or adapters.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because many institutional assistants also use search.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner explains a specialized assistant as durable model adaptation rather than product magic.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Separates base model, fine-tuned model, and runtime retrieval.

questions.question: If fine-tuning is used for alignment, what does that mean about future model behavior?

choices.text: Training can make some instruction-following or preference patterns more likely in later responses.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Fine-tuning can support alignment by durably shaping output patterns.

choices.text: Every future answer is guaranteed safe and true.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Fine-tuning can improve behavior, but it does not guarantee truth or safety.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because alignment sounds like solving behavior.

choices.text: Only the visible system prompt changed for one session.
choices.feedback: Close, but a system prompt steers the current run. Fine-tuning is durable training.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because system prompts can steer behavior strongly.

choices.text: The model acquired human values and moral agency.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. Fine-tuning changes output patterns; it does not create moral agency.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because aligned behavior can sound value-driven.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner connects fine-tuning to alignment without overclaiming morality or guarantees.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Prepares the next card’s alignment distinction.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Reason about alignment as behavior shaping across training, system design, evaluation, and runtime controls without mistaking it for conscience or guaranteed truth.
secondaryObjectives: Separate durable alignment methods from runtime steering.
Distinguish model behavior from moral agency.
Recognize policy filters and guardrails as product/system layers around model output.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Alignment can use fine-tuning, preference optimization, system prompts, policies, filters, and evaluation; it shapes behavior but does not make the model morally aware.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: Alignment makes a model conscious or morally good.
A policy filter is the same as retraining weights.
Aligned output guarantees truth.
learningObjectiveReview.questionCountRationale: Four questions are warranted because alignment mixes durable training, runtime controls, evaluation, and human-use boundaries.

kept: Alignment remains behavior shaping toward instructions, preferences, and safety boundaries.
revised: Moved from one definition question to mechanism-rich scenarios.
added: Durable/runtime distinction, policy-filter scenario, evaluation scenario, and moral-agency misconception check.

questions.question: An alignment-shaped assistant refuses a harmful request and follows a safer instruction instead. What should a model-literate learner conclude?

choices.text: Its behavior was shaped toward instructions and safety boundaries, but that does not prove moral understanding.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Alignment shapes behavior; it is not magic morality.

choices.text: The model must understand morality the way a person does.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. Refusal behavior can be shaped without human moral understanding.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because refusals can sound principled.

choices.text: Every answer from the assistant is now guaranteed true.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Alignment can shape behavior, but it does not guarantee factual truth.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because safer behavior can feel more trustworthy.

choices.text: The LLM stopped using learned weights and became a rulebook.
choices.feedback: Close, but an aligned product can still use a learned model plus rules, filters, or policies.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because aligned products may use rules or filters.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner avoids turning behavior shaping into claims about conscience.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports risk literacy without fear or hype.

questions.question: A product uses instruction fine-tuning, a system prompt, and a policy filter around one LLM. Which alignment map is most useful?

choices.text: Some alignment changes can be durable training changes, while other controls steer or filter the current run.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Alignment can happen through several layers, not one magic switch.

choices.text: Every alignment layer permanently rewrites the model weights.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Fine-tuning may change weights, but system prompts and filters can steer behavior at runtime.
choices.representedMisconception: runtime control vs durable weight update
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because all layers affect visible behavior.

choices.text: Alignment is only a better prompt typed by the user.
choices.feedback: Close, but alignment can include training, policies, evaluation, and runtime controls beyond the user prompt.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because prompts are visible and powerful.

choices.text: Alignment is the same thing as sampling the most probable token.
choices.feedback: Sampling chooses a next token from probabilities. Alignment shapes or constrains behavior around that process.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both affect the final response.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner reasons through a layered product stack.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Clarifies model, policy, prompt, and filter boundaries.

questions.question: Why do teams evaluate aligned models with tests and human feedback after training or deployment?

choices.text: Because shaped behavior can still fail, drift, or create unexpected tradeoffs in new situations.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Alignment needs evaluation; it is not a one-time guarantee.

choices.text: To check whether the model has developed a conscience.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Evaluation checks behavior and outcomes, not inner conscience.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because evaluations judge behavior.

choices.text: Because aligned models no longer need human review.
choices.feedback: This choice overclaims. Aligned systems can still need review, monitoring, and accountability.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because alignment sounds like completion.

choices.text: Because lower training loss alone proves the model is safe.
choices.feedback: Close, but loss alone is not a full safety or usefulness evaluation.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because loss is an important training signal.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner sees evaluation as necessary because behavior shaping can fail in context.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Connects mechanics to responsible human oversight.

questions.question: A guardrail blocks one unsafe response from reaching the user. What changed most directly?

choices.text: A policy or filter layer affected the visible response; that does not necessarily mean the model weights changed.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Guardrails can be runtime controls around a model.

choices.text: The base model was definitely fine-tuned at that moment.
choices.feedback: Not quite. A filter can block output during runtime without retraining the model.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both can change visible behavior.

choices.text: The response became guaranteed factual because it passed a guardrail.
choices.feedback: Passing a guardrail is not the same as being true. Evidence and review can still matter.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because allowed output may feel approved.

choices.text: The model realized the request was wrong.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. The visible behavior can come from shaped model behavior or surrounding controls.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the refusal may sound reflective.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner separates runtime product controls from durable model training.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Builds model-vs-product literacy.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Explain ordinary inference as fixed weights plus temporary context-shaped computation, not durable learning.
secondaryObjectives: Trace current context through temporary activations and hidden states.
Separate forward passes from weight updates.
Connect inference to prompt/response and next-token generation.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Inference uses learned weights to compute temporary internal states and next-token scores; it normally does not update weights or training data.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: Normal chat trains the model.
Inference searches every document the model has seen.
Temporary activations are durable memory.
learningObjectiveReview.questionCountRationale: Four questions fit because inference is a dense gateway concept connecting fixed weights, temporary states, context, and generation.

kept: Inference remains the line between using a model and training it.
revised: Expanded beyond the temporary-activations question.
added: Forward-pass trace, current-context boundary, and ordinary-chat scenario.

questions.question: During ordinary inference, the current context enters the model. What changes temporarily, and what usually stays fixed?

choices.text: Temporary activations and hidden states change; learned weights usually stay fixed.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Inference uses the map without redrawing it.

choices.text: The learned weights change after every user prompt.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Ordinary inference can use context, but it usually does not rewrite weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the model may adapt within a conversation.

choices.text: The training dataset changes to include the new prompt.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. A prompt can be current context without joining the training dataset.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because prompts feel like new information.

choices.text: The model’s conscious attention shifts to the user’s intent.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. Inference computes temporary vectors; it is not conscious attention.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the response may seem attentive.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner identifies temporary internal state versus durable parameters.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Core boundary for later context, attention, and hidden-state lessons.

questions.question: In one inference step, which trace best matches the model’s next-token path?

choices.text: Context enters a forward pass, temporary states form, logits are produced, and sampling can choose a token.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. That is the live next-token path through inference.

choices.text: Example enters training, loss updates weights, and a future model checkpoint is saved.
choices.feedback: Close, but that is a training path. Inference uses existing weights to produce the next-token scores.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because it is also a model computation path.

choices.text: Retriever searches outside documents, then the model permanently learns the snippets.
choices.feedback: Retrieval can add context before inference, but it does not by itself make the model permanently learn snippets.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because retrieval may happen before generation.

choices.text: Tokenizer splits text and the answer appears without model layers.
choices.feedback: Tokenization prepares input, but inference still runs model layers to produce scores.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because tokenization is an early required step.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner orders the live generation mechanism rather than recalling a label.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Connects inference to hidden states, logits, sampling, and autoregression.

questions.question: A chatbot remembers an earlier line in the same conversation and uses it in the next answer. What is the best model-level explanation?

choices.text: The earlier line is still in the current context, so fixed weights can use it during inference.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Context can feel like memory while still being temporary input.

choices.text: The earlier line was written into the model’s weights.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Same-conversation context is temporary input, not necessarily a weight update.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the model appears to remember.

choices.text: The chatbot fine-tuned itself on the earlier line.
choices.feedback: Close, but adaptation within a context window is not the same as fine-tuning.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the answer adapts to the conversation.

choices.text: The model searched every document it was pretrained on.
choices.feedback: This describes search better than ordinary inference. The model can use visible context without searching training sources.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the answer may include earlier information.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner distinguishes context-shaped inference from durable memory.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Reduces fear that every chat writes into the base model.

questions.question: If ordinary inference normally does not update weights, what follows for future conversations?

choices.text: A useful answer now does not automatically teach the base model to behave differently tomorrow.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Inference can be useful without being durable training.

choices.text: Every good answer automatically becomes permanent skill.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Durable skill changes require training or another model-update process.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the response looks like successful practice.

choices.text: The current prompt cannot affect the answer at all.
choices.feedback: Close, but fixed weights can still respond differently to different current contexts.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because weights stay fixed.

choices.text: There is no need to review outputs because inference is temporary.
choices.feedback: This choice overclaims. Temporary outputs can still affect people and decisions, so review can matter.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because temporary sounds harmless.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner reasons from the no-weight-update boundary to future behavior.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Connects mechanics to practical use and review.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Separate given prompt/context tokens from generated response tokens and explain append-and-repeat generation.
secondaryObjectives: Identify prompt tokens as provided context.
Identify response-so-far as generated tokens now included in context.
Explain why the model does not generate a full answer all at once.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Prompt tokens are given; response tokens are generated one at a time, appended, and used as context for the next inference step.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: The whole response appears at once.
Generated response tokens permanently train the model.
Prompt and response are the same kind of user-provided input.
learningObjectiveReview.questionCountRationale: Three questions cover the given/generated boundary, append-and-repeat trace, and a common permanent-learning misconception.

kept: Prompt vs response remains the given-context versus generated-token distinction.
revised: Expanded beyond one append question.

questions.question: A user gives a complete prompt, then the model begins writing response tokens. Which boundary matters most?

choices.text: Prompt tokens are given to the model; response tokens are generated and then appended to context.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Prompt is given; response is generated and appended.

choices.text: Both prompt and response tokens were typed by the user.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The user provides the prompt; the model generates response tokens.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both can appear together in the conversation transcript.

choices.text: The model writes the whole response at once after reading the prompt.
choices.feedback: Close, but the model generates response tokens step by step.
choices.representedMisconception: autoregressive generation vs full answer at once
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the interface may show a smooth answer.

choices.text: Each response token permanently updates the model’s weights.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Response tokens can shape the current run without updating weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because response-so-far shapes later tokens.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner separates given input from generated output in the same context.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports later tokenization and autoregression lessons.

questions.question: After the model chooses one next response token, what does the next inference step see?

choices.text: The original prompt plus the response so far, including the newly appended token.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. The context grows as generated response tokens are appended.

choices.text: Only the newly chosen token, with the prompt removed.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The next step can use the prompt and response-so-far that remain in context.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the new token is the most recent event.

choices.text: A permanent memory of the token stored in model weights.
choices.feedback: Close, but using a token later in the run is context, not a weight update.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the model uses the new token later.

choices.text: No context, because softmax disappears after one token.
choices.feedback: Softmax can help choose a token, but the chosen token can still be appended to the next context.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because softmax is a later token-choice mechanism.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner traces append-and-repeat rather than reading a definition.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Prepares context window and autoregression.

questions.question: A generated response improves after the user adds a clarifying sentence. What changed most directly?

choices.text: The current prompt/context changed, so the next response tokens were generated from better input.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Better context can improve the current response without training the model.

choices.text: The model was fine-tuned by the clarifying sentence.
choices.feedback: Not quite. A clarifying sentence can steer the current run; fine-tuning is additional training.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the answer improves after feedback.

choices.text: The model understood its mistake like a person and decided to improve.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. Better output can come from better context.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the interaction resembles human revision.

choices.text: Prompts cannot affect generated response tokens.
choices.feedback: Close, but fixed weights still process different prompts into different response probabilities.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because weights stay fixed during inference.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner distinguishes current-context improvement from durable model learning.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Connects prompt literacy to model mechanics.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Explain tokenization as the split from text into model-readable chunks that may not match human words.
secondaryObjectives: Recognize tokens as words, word pieces, punctuation, spaces, or other chunks.
Avoid treating tokens as permanent memories or concepts.
Connect tokens to token IDs and embeddings.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: Tokenization is a representation step before token IDs and embedding lookup; it does not update model weights or guarantee meaning.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: Tokens are always whole words.
Tokens are the same as concepts.
Tokenization stores memory.
learningObjectiveReview.questionCountRationale: Three questions cover chunk shape, representation sequence, and the token-versus-meaning misconception.

revised: Expanded beyond one “what can a token be” item.
added: Uneven split scenario and token-to-ID bridge.

questions.question: A tokenizer splits “startled.” into pieces like “start”, “led”, and “.” What should that tell a learner?

choices.text: Tokens can be uneven text chunks, including word pieces and punctuation.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Tokens are model-readable chunks, not always whole words.

choices.text: Every token must be a complete dictionary word.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Tokenizers can split words, punctuation, spaces, or other chunks.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because many displayed tokens look word-like.

choices.text: Each token is a permanent memory stored in the model.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. A token is a representation chunk, not memory by itself.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because tokens are reused in later processing.

choices.text: Each token is exactly one human concept.
choices.feedback: Close, but token boundaries do not perfectly match human concepts or meanings.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because tokens often carry meaning clues.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner interprets a concrete tokenizer split.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Prepares token ID and embedding distinctions.

questions.question: Before transformer layers process text, which tokens-to-IDs-to-embeddings path comes first?

choices.text: Text is split into tokens, tokens map to token IDs, and IDs look up embeddings.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Tokenization starts the bridge from text to numerical computation.

choices.text: Raw English sentences move through every layer without numerical representation.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The model needs numerical representations, starting with tokens and IDs.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because users see text at the interface.

choices.text: Weights are rewritten before text becomes tokens.
choices.feedback: Close, but tokenization prepares the input; it does not rewrite weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because weights are central to the model.

choices.text: The model generates the finished response before tokenization happens.
choices.feedback: Generation also uses tokens. Text needs token representation before model layers can process it.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because users notice the final text first.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner orders the representation pipeline.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Connects tokenization to token IDs and embeddings.

questions.question: Both the user prompt and the generated response eventually appear as text. How does tokenization treat them?

choices.text: Prompt text and generated response text can both be represented as tokens.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Tokenization applies to text entering and leaving the generation loop.

choices.text: Only prompt text becomes tokens; response text skips token IDs.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Generated response tokens are token IDs before being displayed as text.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the user sees the response as text.

choices.text: Only response text becomes tokens; prompts stay as raw English.
choices.feedback: Close, but prompts also need token representation before model processing.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because prompts feel natural to humans.

choices.text: Tokenizing either one permanently trains the model.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Tokenization represents text; it does not update weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because tokenization is a required model step.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner connects tokenization to prompt/response distinction.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports the full day-in-the-life trace.

learningObjectiveReview.primaryLearningObjective: Explain token IDs as lookup numbers that point from token chunks to learned embedding vectors, not meanings by themselves.
secondaryObjectives: Separate token ID from token text and meaning.
Connect token IDs to embedding table lookup.
Recognize generated response tokens as token IDs before display.
learningObjectiveReview.modelMechanismOrBoundary: A token ID is a stable lookup key in a tokenizer/model setup; using it retrieves embeddings but does not guarantee truth or update weights.
mythsMisconceptionsToCorrect: The ID number is the meaning.
Token IDs store permanent memory.
Token IDs skip embeddings or tensors.
learningObjectiveReview.questionCountRationale: Three questions cover lookup-key mechanics, meaning misconception, and generated-token flow.

revised: Expanded beyond a single embedding lookup question.
added: Meaning boundary and response-token ID scenario.

questions.question: After tokenization, the token “dog” maps to an integer ID. What does the model use that ID for next?

choices.text: To look up a learned starting vector in the embedding table.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. The ID is a lookup key for an embedding vector.

choices.text: To store the complete meaning of dog inside the number itself.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The number itself is not the meaning; it points to learned numerical patterns.
choices.representedMisconception: ID number vs learned meaning pattern
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the ID consistently points to that token.

choices.text: To permanently remember this user’s sentence.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Token IDs represent chunks; they do not store a user memory.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because IDs are reusable in model processing.

choices.text: To skip embeddings and tensors entirely.
choices.feedback: Close, but the ID is a lookup key; embeddings and tensors still carry numerical representation forward.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the ID is already numeric.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner links token ID to embedding lookup instead of meaning or memory.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Prepares embeddings and tensors.

questions.question: A learner says “982 means cat because the ID is the meaning.” What correction is most model-literate?

choices.text: 982 is a lookup number for a token; meaning comes from learned patterns around embeddings and model layers.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. The ID points into the system; it is not understanding by itself.

choices.text: 982 means cat because the model consciously understands the number.
choices.feedback: That gives the model too much mind. A lookup number does not imply conscious understanding.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the model uses the ID fluently.

choices.text: 982 guarantees every sentence about cats will be true.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Token IDs do not guarantee factual output.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the token has a stable identity.

choices.text: 982 is randomly redefined every time the user sends a prompt.
choices.feedback: Close, but token ID mappings are fixed for a tokenizer/model setup; sampling randomness is a different step.
choices.representedMisconception: stable tokenizer mapping vs per-run randomness
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because sampling can be random later.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner corrects the ID-is-meaning misconception.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Clarifies representation before embeddings.

questions.question: During generation, the model chooses a next response token ID before the user sees text. What happens after that?

choices.text: The chosen ID can be displayed as text and appended as a response token in the current context.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Generated tokens are IDs before they become visible text.

choices.text: The chosen ID permanently updates the model weights.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The chosen token can affect the next context without changing weights.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the token affects the next step.

choices.text: The model skips IDs and chooses only raw human words.
choices.feedback: Close, but the model works with token IDs before text is displayed.
choices.representedMisconception: token IDs vs raw text generation
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because users see words on screen.

choices.text: The ID retrieves the original training document for that word.
choices.feedback: This describes retrieval better than token ID lookup. Token IDs point to token representations, not original documents.
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because lookup sounds like fetching a source.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner connects token IDs back to response-token generation.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Completes the early representation chain.
